title: Styling Conversation 2 — build planning: ui-PRD-v0.6, implementation plan, six slice prompts, version-bundling resolution
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: not a formal kickoff document — this conversation opened against the styling design pass committed earlier the same day (styling-design-pass.md, output of SES-027) plus the workstream plan at styling-workstream-plan.md. Doug initiated by saying 'lets do the styling conversation' after the prior turn had reconstructed which conversation produced what (he had been confused which conversation gave him 'this script' — turned out to be the styling design pass document, not a script; the conversation had been searching past chats to disambiguate).

Pre-flight. Sparse clone of crmbuilder repo with CLAUDE.md, PRDs/, and crmbuilder-v2/ source. Read CLAUDE.md (684 lines) to ground architecture and conventions. Read styling-workstream-plan.md (227 lines) end to end. Confirmed no styling-conversation-2-kickoff.md existed — working from the workstream plan and design-pass document directly. Verified database state at conversation open: latest session SES-027, latest decision DEC-094, latest PI PI-017. Next anticipated identifiers: SES-028, DEC-095/096/097. (This identifier anticipation later proved wrong; see close-out section.)

What Conversation 2 owed per the workstream plan: (1) settle the version-bundling question — v0.5 bundled vs v0.6 separate; (2) produce a styling release PRD; (3) produce an implementation plan; (4) produce slice build prompts.

Decision 1 — Version bundling (DEC-105). Pause with full eight-element template. Two viable options: bundle the styling work into v0.5 (one release for engagement management + visual design system) versus ship as separate v0.6 release. Option A (bundle) gave one closeout cycle and slice-name collisions could be managed via prefix; Option B (separate) gave two closeout cycles but kept release versions tied to functional themes (the v2 history-navigation index that 'what changed in v0.5' answers cleanly). Doug chose B (separate v0.6). Reasoning: release versions are v2's primary navigation index (git log, README, decisions table, __version__, MCP-exposed status); v2 to date had held one clean functional theme per release (v0.1 storage, v0.2 base CRUD, v0.3 references + sessions write surface, v0.4 polish + methodology panels); bundling would make v0.5 the first v2 release with two unrelated themes welded together, permanently blurring navigability. Cost: one additional closeout cycle (~half day of administrative work). The workstream plan author's weak prior leaned this way and was ratified. Follow-on detail committed in the same decision: PRD file ui-PRD-v0.6.md; implementation plan ui-v0.6-implementation-plan.md; slice prompts under prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.6-{A..F}-*.md; __version__ after v0.6 closeout '0.6.0'; v0.5 ships first (engagement is currently slightly ahead in planning depth); engagement panel coupling per workstream plan §4's 'v0.5 ships first' branch; design-pass decisions stand as-is.

Decision 2 — Six-slice structure (DEC-106). Surfaced as draft rather than full template (substantively a packaging choice rather than two genuinely different visual outcomes). The workstream plan §5.3 sketched five slices (Foundation / Governance retrofit / Methodology retrofit / Dialog polish / Closeout). Drafted six slices for v0.6: A Foundation + About dialog (canary surface exercising the full token system end-to-end); B Sidebar + master-pane delegate (the shared selected-state vocabulary); C Panel retrofits + ReferencesSection (all 12-13 panels in one omnibus retrofit slice); D Dialogs + form controls; E Status + crash banner (separated from dialogs because crash banner and status surfaces are distinct concerns from dialog chrome); F Closeout. Doug approved as drafted. Three reconciliation differences from the strawman: (1) master-pane delegate pulled to its own slice B rather than buried in panel retrofits — the delegate is shared across all 12+ panels and putting it in panel-retrofit slices would force artificial governance/methodology coupling; (2) governance and methodology retrofits collapsed into single omnibus slice C — the retrofit pattern is identical across all panels, and splitting repeats review work without buying anything once slice B has landed the shared delegate; (3) status surfaces + crash banner promoted to their own slice E — keeping two distinct concerns separately reviewable.

Decision 3 — Slice acceptance pattern (DEC-107). Drafted briefly rather than full template (visual work has different acceptance signals than functional work; the choice was between several reasonable approaches with similar value). topics_covered:

This conversation built the foundational layer of v0.6 styling: the token system, asset bundling for fonts and icons, modal elevation infrastructure, and a re-skinned About dialog acting as a canary exercise of the new token plumbing end-to-end.

Token system rewrite in ui/styling.py replaced the 72-line v0.1 minimal QSS stub from DEC-024 with a full theme-keyed TOKENS dict (per DEC-089), a t() accessor, a build_app_stylesheet() generator, and an apply_stylesheet(app) entry point. The light theme codifies every value from styling-design-pass.md §1 (color, space, font, radius, border, shadow tokens). Legacy module constants (ACCENT_COLOR, ACCENT_HOVER, ERROR_TEXT_COLOR, DEFAULT_FONT_FAMILY, DEFAULT_FONT_POINT_SIZE) were removed; consumers in panels/engagements.py migrated alongside splash.py.

Asset bundling under ui/assets/ added Inter Variable v4.1 + JetBrains Mono Variable v2.304 per DEC-090 (OFL license bundled) and the initial 14-SVG Lucide icon wave per DEC-092 (ISC license bundled). The ui/icons.py loader exposes lucide(name, size, color_token) with runtime SVG color tinting and (name, size, color_token) caching. Modal elevation per DEC-091 landed as ui/elevation.py (apply_dialog_shadow helper using QGraphicsDropShadowEffect) plus ui/widgets/modal_backdrop.py (ModalBackdropOverlay with attach/detach module functions, fade-in via QPropertyAnimation on windowOpacity). Five modal dialogs received per-dialog hooks: crud_dialog (covers CRUD + CRUD-delete), versioned_replace_dialog (covers Charter + Status replace), about_dialog, dialogs/error, dialogs/reference_delete. list_detail_panel.py got chrome-only hooks (objectName, splitter handle, 45/55 default split) since it is not modal.

Notable finding: Inter Variable v4.x registers under the family name 'Inter Variable' rather than the expected 'Inter' — ui/app.py needs an Inter → 'Inter Variable' substitution after QFontDatabase loads bundled fonts; without it, the project QSS that references 'Inter' would fall through to the system fallback. About dialog re-skin per design pass §2.8 and DEC-094 restructured to wordmark + tagline header + vertical two-line metadata list + single right-aligned Close button; serves as the canary surface exercising the full token system end-to-end. test_about_dialog.py updated to walk the new vertical layout and to match the 'API base url' sentence-cased label. title: v0.6 slice A follow-up — force Fusion style + explicit light palette to neutralize OS theme bleed-through
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.6-A-followup-force-light-palette.md. This conversation diagnosed and fixed a defect surfaced during slice B visual review: on Linux Mint with a Cinnamon/GTK theme, Qt picked up GTK palette values for widget surfaces the project QSS did not explicitly cover (QLabel backgrounds, QWidget container backgrounds), so on a dark Mint theme the app rendered black backgrounds with near-black text — 'dark blue on black' — unreadable labels across the UI.

Diagnosis surfaced that simply changing the OS theme did not reliably fix it; some Mint 'light' themes still have dark palette roles, and Qt's GTK reading was sensitive to the specific theme structure. The root cause was that slice A's build_application() constructed QApplication, loaded fonts, and applied the project QSS but did not force a Qt style or palette. Without a forced style, Qt delegated to the platform-native style on Linux which read GTK palette values; without a forced palette, the default QPalette inherited dark theme roles where the OS theme provided them.

The surgical fix: force QStyle to Fusion (cross-platform Qt-native style that does not delegate to GTK or native platform styles), then force QPalette to an explicit light palette built from TOKENS['light'] so widget surfaces without explicit QSS rules render in design-pass light-theme colors. Palette role-to-token mapping mirrors design pass §1.2 color tokens; no design pass values are duplicated or drifted. After the fix, the application renders identically regardless of OS theme.

Notable findings: (1) the dark-on-dark failure mode was OS-theme-bleed-through, not a TOKENS values bug — the design pass palette was correct; it was the unstyled surfaces that needed explicit palette protection; (2) apply_stylesheet wraps the style in Qt's internal QStyleSheetStyle proxy, so a naive style().objectName() assertion sees 'QStyleSheetStyle' rather than 'Fusion' — the regression test test_style_is_fusion clears the project QSS before asserting on style().objectName() to expose the underlying base Fusion style. Intent of the test is preserved with this contortion documented in a comment. Five regression-test cases added in tests/crmbuilder_v2/ui/test_app_palette.py: five palette role assertions plus the Fusion style assertion, with the WindowText/Window contrast case (the label-readability bug) explicitly tested as test_palette_window_text_is_dark_neutral. Test count: 1236 baseline + 5 new = 1241 passed, 1 skipped.

title: v0.6 slice D — dialogs and form controls (button categories, edit-dialog context strip, delete-confirm)
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.6-D-dialogs-and-form-controls.md. This conversation delivered the dialog + form-control vocabulary that defines v0.6's interactive surfaces: five button categories with full state coverage, helpers that wrap them, the internal edit-dialog context strip per design pass §2.7, and the migration of every panel's New + Delete + Add-reference + refresh CTAs.

Button categories landed in build_app_stylesheet additions: Primary (accent fill, white text), Secondary (transparent + neutral border — default), Destructive (danger fill), Text/Link (transparent + accent text), Icon-only (28x28 square, icon-only) — each with full state coverage (hover/pressed/disabled/focused). The buttonCategory dynamic property drives the QSS rules; default fall-through ensures a bare QPushButton renders as Secondary so no panel accidentally renders an unstyled button.

Button helpers in widgets/form_helpers.py wrap the categories: primary_button(text), destructive_button(text), text_link_button(text, *, icon_name=None), icon_button(icon_name, *, tooltip). Migration was systematic across the codebase: Dialog Save → primary_button via the CRUD base + replace base; Dialog Delete → destructive_button via CRUD-delete base + ReferenceDeleteDialog; per-record detail-pane Delete → destructive_button across 13 panels; panel 'New X' toolbar CTAs → primary_button across 13 panels; ReferencesSection 'Add reference' → text_link_button(plus); panel toolbar refresh → icon_button(rotate-ccw). Legacy #c1272d red + #b6868a disabled-destructive treatment removed from reference_delete.py and crud_dialog.py.

Combo dropdown + checkbox styling additions to build_app_stylesheet: QComboBox::down-arrow with a pre-tinted Lucide chevron-down (color.neutral.500); QComboBox QAbstractItemView popup styling (tokenized container, per-item padding, hover/selected states); QCheckBox::indicator with Lucide check.svg for checked state — pre-tinted SVG variants committed to assets (check-neutral-0 for checked, check-neutral-300 for disabled-checked).

Internal edit-dialog context strip in base/crud_dialog.py: new _build_context_strip() on EntityCrudDialog auto-applied in edit mode only. Renders color.neutral.100 background with mono identifier + body name. Explicitly excluded from create-mode, delete-confirm, About, Error, ReferenceDelete, ReferenceCreate, VersionedReplace dialogs per design pass §2.7. Delete-confirm dialog body treatment got font.line.relaxed (1.6) line height via a role='delete-body' QSS rule, with the Destructive button right-aligned and Cancel to its left in both EntityCrudDeleteDialog and ReferenceDeleteDialog.

Test update: test_list_detail_panel.py's refresh-button assertion now reads tooltip + buttonCategory property instead of visible text (icon-only buttons carry no label, so text-based assertion no longer works). Full v2 UI suite green (633 passed).

topics_covered:
Seed prompt: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.6-E-status-and-crash-banner.md. This conversation delivered the four small status/error/warning surfaces that complete v0.6 visual coherence: an inline WarningCallout widget, the Processes soft-deleted-domain warning retoken'd, the error dialog header retoken'd, and the crash banner re-skinned.

WarningCallout widget at ui/widgets/warning_callout.py is a new inline panel-level warning row per design pass §2.9 — a Lucide circle-alert icon plus color.warning.default text in a horizontal row. Reusable; current single caller is the Processes panel's soft-deleted-domain warning. A QSS rule for QLabel#warningCalloutText (small + color.warning.default) lives in build_app_stylesheet.

Processes panel retoken in ui/panels/processes.py removed the legacy _WARNING_STYLE = 'color: #B22222;' constant. The soft-deleted-domain warning now renders via WarningCallout (warning-amber, not danger-red) — because the message is informational about a removed-but-still-referenced domain, not a hard error per design pass §2.9. The ad-hoc '⚠' character prefix dropped; the Lucide icon carries the affordance now.

Error dialog header retoken in ui/dialogs/error.py removed the legacy _BANNER_STYLE = 'color: #1F3864; font-weight: bold;' constant (a v0.1 vestige using the legacy navy). Header restructured: leading Lucide circle-x icon at 18px color.danger.default + title in font.size.heading_3 semibold color.danger.text via a QLabel#errorDialogHeader QSS rule. Body content unchanged.

Crash banner re-skin in ui/crash_banner.py removed _BANNER_BACKGROUND and bespoke button-style constants. Background color.danger.default; text color.neutral.0 white at font.size.body font.weight.medium; leading Lucide circle-alert icon at 16px white. Padding 12px x 16px (space.3 x space.4). Banner button styling kept as per-widget setStyleSheet (the banner is exceptional — semi-transparent white-on-color styling doesn't fit any of the five design-system button categories from slice D). setAutoFillBackground + palette ensure the danger background renders reliably on the top-level banner widget. Behavior unchanged: always-on-top, full window width, persists until dismissed or error resolved.

Inline form-field error verification: the QLabel[role='error'] QSS rule from slice A confirmed rendering correctly across multiple panels' validation flows. Test update: tests/crmbuilder_v2/ui/test_processes_panel.py's soft-deleted-domain warning assertions look up the warning by its new WarningCallout widget class instead of QLabel; text content assertion preserved (the widget exposes text() with the message body). No schema changes; no API changes; v0.5 test suite remains green.

title: v0.6 slice F — closeout (release 0.6.0): version bump, README, WCAG contrast build gate, design pass §4.4 traceability fix
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.6-F-closeout.md. This conversation mechanically completed the v0.6 release that discharges PI-001 after four prior deferrals (DEC-024, DEC-026, DEC-037, DEC-042 → DEC-076 reopening): version bump, README release note, WCAG contrast test as build gate, design pass §4.4 traceability fix.

Version bump: crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/__init__.py __version__ = '0.6.0'. Root pyproject.toml is the v1 distribution; the v2 version source per CLAUDE.md is __version__ only (About dialog reads via importlib.metadata for 'crmbuilder-v2' which is never installed as a standalone dist, so the __version__ fallback always wins). README v0.6 release note section added matching v0.5's format, placed above v0.5 in chronological-newest-first order. Highlights: tokens module (60+ tokens), bundled Inter + JetBrains Mono + Lucide assets, shared selected-state vocabulary, panel retrofit, dialog work, status surfaces (slice E), modal elevation, WCAG contrast verification, retired legacy colors.

WCAG contrast test at tests/crmbuilder_v2/ui/test_token_contrast.py is the build gate per DEC-107: 8 text-on-background combinations from design pass §4.4 (A9) verified against WCAG AA threshold (4.5:1 for small body text); plus one helper-sanity test (black-on-white = 21:1, identical = 1:1) totaling 9 cases. Hand-rolled WCAG 2.x luminance + contrast implementation since the wcag-contrast-ratio PyPI lib is not bundled and the formula is short enough to inline.

Notable finding: the WCAG test surfaced two design-pass transcription errors that needed correcting. color.neutral.500: drafted '#7A8694' computed 3.71:1 on white (the design pass claimed 4.62:1) — failed AA at small body size; adjusted to '#6A7480' which computes 4.75:1 (passes AA, matches design intent). color.warning.default: drafted '#B0731A' computed 3.95:1 on white (the design pass claimed ~4.7) — failed AA at body size; adjusted to '#9D6517' which computes 4.88:1 (passes AA). The color.warning.default adjustment was the explicit recommendation in design pass §4.4 and PRD Open Question #4, validated empirically here.

Design pass update: §4.4 (A9) both adjusted ratios noted inline with their old drafted values for traceability; closing line now points at the slice F WCAG build gate as the authoritative verification. Test update: tests/crmbuilder_v2/ui/test_about_dialog.py test_version_displays_* renamed and asserts '0.6.0'. Full regression: uv run pytest tests/crmbuilder_v2/ -v returns green across the entire suite (1250 passed, 1 skipped — the same v0.5 test inventory plus the 9 new WCAG cases). v0.6 IS RELEASED. PI-001 discharged. No schema changes; no API changes.

title: Multi-tenancy routing fix — planning: seven architectural decisions, two-slice build plan, slice prompts authored
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/multi-tenancy-routing-fix-planning-kickoff.md — the planning conversation kickoff produced earlier the same day. The kickoff names the workstream's purpose (fix Bug 1 + Bug 2), the read-first companion (multi-tenancy-routing-investigation-report.md), seven specific Phase 1 architectural questions to surface via the eight-element template, and Phase 2 deliverables (slice plan, per-slice Claude Code prompts, planning item PI-021 [originally PI-018, rebased at apply time — see end-of-topics note], close-out artifacts).

Pre-flight. Loaded crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md via github:get_file_contents (sparse-clone constraint: Claude.ai sandbox has no local clone access, so all repo reads went through the GitHub API with the userMemories' stored PAT). Loaded the investigation report similarly. Confirmed the report's sections A–G layout: §A API startup DB resolution, §B export hook render-target resolution, §C shared resolver gap, §D runtime engagement switch capability, §E proposed fix paths, §F shared workstream framing, §G seven additional findings.

Decision 1 — Fresh-install behavior with no current_engagement.json (DEC-108). Surfaced via full eight-element template per profile preferences (real downstream impact + multiple viable options with meaningfully different outcomes). Three options weighed: (a) auto-pick if exactly one engagement; (b) fail loud always; (c) hybrid first-time-only auto-pick. Doug chose (b) fail loud always. Reasoning: the bug being fixed is silent fallback to v2.db; the fix shouldn't introduce a new silent fallback under a different name. (c)'s 'first invocation only' rule creates state operators can't reason about from filesystem inspection. (a) is least-worst of the auto-pick options but still silent at exactly the moment loudness is wanted. Follow-on detail captured: error message text 'No active engagement. Activate one via the desktop UI's Engagements panel, or pass --engagement <code> when running the API standalone.' Exit code 2 (configuration error). Marker-points-at-missing-code message names the missing code: 'Active engagement \'X\' not found in meta DB.' Error written to stderr via print(..., file=sys.stderr) AND stdout so the UI subprocess capture sees it. Before uvicorn binds the port.

Decision 2 — Missing engagement_export_dir behavior (DEC-109). Surfaced via full eight-element template. Two options: (a) refuse the write; (b) per-engagement scratch dir under data/engagements/{code}/db-export/. Doug chose (a) refuse the write. Reasoning: same as Decision 1 — the bug being fixed is silent fallback, and a scratch dir is silent fallback by a different name. Operators who legitimately don't know where they want exports yet can either set the export_dir to a temp path now and update later, or hold off on commits until they decide — both are deliberate acts. The Open Engagement flow is the right place to surface unconfigured export_dirs; the UI can warn at activation time so the operator sees it before their first commit. Follow-on detail: the gate goes in the new route_settings_to_engagement(code) helper; helper sets CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR to literal sentinel '__UNCONFIGURED__' when meta DB has null; new exception EngagementExportDirNotConfigured raised at write time by the gate helper; access/db.py:session_scope and force_export raise before write_staging; UI Open Engagement flow surfaces yellow warning band when activating engagement with no export_dir. Empty string treated as null throughout.

Decision 3 — Env var vs direct Settings override (DEC-110). Surfaced via full eight-element template. The deepest architectural decision in the bunch. Two options: (a) env var + cache reset (matches existing CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH pattern); (b) Settings becomes engagement-aware directly with active_engagement_code field and computed properties. Doug chose (a). Reasoning: smaller diff (~5 files vs ~10+); matches existing pattern operators and the codebase already know; doesn't dig into the chicken-and-egg between Settings.db_path → data_dir() → meta_db_path(); option (b) ends up being option (a) plus a property-based facade because subprocess inheritance still requires an env var or CLI flag to communicate active engagement across the process boundary. Option (b) is the right shape for a v0.6+ architecture-cleanup workstream if Settings' role expands further; premature for a bug-fix slice. Follow-on detail: helper signature route_settings_to_engagement(code: str) -> None; raises UnknownEngagementError if code not in meta DB; cache resets in order reset_settings_cache → reset_engine_cache → reset_meta_engine_cache (last only when data_dir() would resolve differently); CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR added to Settings as sibling of CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH via the same env_prefix mechanism (config.py:24); empty-string treated as unset; sentinel '__UNCONFIGURED__' set when null in meta DB; session_scope checks for sentinel and raises before write_staging.

Decision 4 — --engagement <code> CLI flag (DEC-111). Surfaced via full eight-element template (operationally lower-stakes than 1–3 but a real scope decision). Two options: (a) land in slice A; (b) defer to follow-on. Doug chose (a). Reasoning: cost is small (~20 LOC + tests); makes Decision 1's error message honest as written (the error references the flag); eliminates one operationally-awkward step from every apply-prompt pre-flight from now on; already in workstream scope per Investigation §F. Deferring would mean either reworking Decision 1's error message now or shipping an error that points at a not-yet-existing flag. Follow-on detail: positional value 'crmbuilder-v2-api --engagement CRMBUILDER'; unknown code → exit 2 with 'Unknown engagement \'<code>\'. Available: CRMBUILDER, CBM.' (enumerated from meta DB); flag-vs-marker disagreement → flag wins, yellow log line emitted '--engagement <flag> overrides current_engagement.json (<marker>)'; agreement → no log line; flag is ephemeral (does not persist to current_engagement.json); tests: happy path, unknown code, flag-overrides-marker, marker-without-flag.

Decision 5 — /active-engagement and/or /admin/runtime-info API endpoints (DEC-112). Surfaced via full eight-element template. Three options: (a) add /admin/runtime-info in slice A; (b) narrower /active-engagement in slice A; (c) defer entirely. Doug chose (c) defer entirely. Reasoning: Investigation §G.6 explicitly flagged it as 'quality-of-life follow-on, not load-bearing'; nothing in the bug fix needs it; pre-flights have a working pattern via filesystem reads of current_engagement.json; the follow-on PI is cheap; the diagnostic surface can be designed properly when there's a real need driving the schema rather than committing to a response shape speculatively. Decision 4 is different because Decision 1's error message references the flag (forcing function); no forcing function here. Follow-on detail: file a follow-on quality-of-life PI (PI-022 or whatever's next-available when the need actually arises); slice A close-out adds this as a NOTE in the slice plan's Deferred section. NOTE: PI numbering for that follow-on deferred to whenever it is actually authored; not opened in this session's payload.

Decision 6 — Catalog exports, force_export, bootstrap/migrate scope (DEC-113). Surfaced via full eight-element template. Three options: (a) centralized gate applied to all active write paths via assert_export_dir_ready helper; (b) defensive per-site inline gates; (c) active-path only, no shared helper. Doug chose (a). Reasoning: today's export_dir bug is one class of bug with four manifestations (session_scope, force_export, catalog exporter, possibly bootstrap/migrate); if we only gate session_scope, three latent regressions remain in the codebase; the cost of the centralized helper is one new function (~10 LOC) + three call sites — strictly less than the cost of three separate inline gates; option (c) is the 'minimum slice surface' instinct, option (a) is the 'fix the class of bug' instinct. Catalog exports specifically MUST be fixed because they're on the active path (Investigation §G.1 was explicit); force_export is free to add since the helper exists; bootstrap/migrate gets a 10-minute read-the-code pass and a one-line conditional fix per branch (engine-scoped → hardcode path; engagement-scoped → apply gate). Follow-on detail: helper signature assert_export_dir_configured(s: Settings) -> None (later widened to assert_export_dir_ready(s) carrying both responsibilities from Decisions 2 and 7); lives in runtime/engagement_routing.py; call sites session_scope before write_staging, force_export before write_staging, catalog exporter's path-return site; about_dialog reads s.export_dir raw with display-mapping for sentinel and missing-path states (does NOT gate); bootstrap/migrate.py path-shape read during slice A.

Decision 7 — Fail-loud vs auto-create at write time (DEC-114). Surfaced via full eight-element template. Three options: (a) fail loud if engagement_export_dir doesn't exist on disk; (b) continue auto-creating full depth; (c) auto-create leaf only. Doug chose (a). Reasoning: aligns with the philosophy of Decisions 1, 2, 6 — no silent fallbacks, no silent rogue-tree creation; today's mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) is Investigation §G.7's 'doubly insidious' finding (writes go to wrong place AND wrong place gets auto-created on demand); Settings-route fix corrects the routing logic but leaves silent-mkdir-on-typo behavior untouched without this decision. The configured engagement_export_dir is supposed to point at an actual existing directory in a real client repo — it's a target operators chose, not a target the system invents. (c) is over-engineered — the parent-vs-leaf rule is one more thing to explain. Operators handle the one-time mkdir-per-engagement setup cost. Follow-on detail: exception EngagementExportDirMissing; message 'Configured engagement_export_dir does not exist on disk: <path>. Either create the directory or update the engagement via Edit Engagement.'; gate consolidated into assert_export_dir_ready(s) carrying both Decision 2 and Decision 7 checks; subdirectory creation below the root keeps using mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) which is safe and useful; about_dialog growth (✓/✗ indicator) deferred to future polish, not slice B.

Phase 2 — slice count. After Phase 1 settled, the slice count question collapsed to '2 slices' rather than requiring its own decision: Decisions 4, 6, 7 all fit in slice A (backend helpers, gate, CLI, tests); Decision 5 deferred; no slice C required. Investigation §F estimated 2 slices; the planning conversation confirmed.

Phase 2 — slice plan authored. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/multi-tenancy-routing-fix-slice-plan.md authored end-to-end (≈10 sections + ASCII directory tree). Slice A scope: extract helpers, gate, CLI routing, fail-loud, catalog/force_export/bootstrap consumer-site fixes, tests — 13 acceptance criteria (A1–A13). Slice B scope: UI refactor of _route_api_at_active_engagement and ActiveEngagementContext to use shared helpers, warning bands on Open Engagement flow, Edit Engagement dialog visual emphasis, about_dialog (not configured) / (missing) display, error-dialog handling for new exceptions, pytest-qt integration tests across two-engagement state — 9 acceptance criteria (B1–B9). Strictly sequential A → B; slice A independently shippable.

Phase 2 — slice prompts authored. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-multi-tenancy-routing-fix-A-helpers-cli-gate.md and CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-multi-tenancy-routing-fix-B-ui-refactor-affordances.md authored as DETAIL-mode pure-execution prompts following the established slice-prompt pattern (pre-flight, numbered workflow steps each with an acceptance check, acceptance gate, commit message scaffold, done). Slice A prompt has 9 workflow steps. Slice B prompt has 7 workflow steps.

Phase 2 — close-out artifacts. ses_044.json (this payload) and CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-044.md authored alongside. Identifier check at close-out artifact authoring time: latest applied SES is SES-043 per the user's kickoff (recovery sequence pre-flight 2), so SES-044 is next-available; latest DEC at conversation open per Investigation references was DEC-107 (the SES-043 payload references it), so DEC-108..114 is next-available for seven decisions; PI-017 was latest per userMemories ('Pending PRDs: Dues, SME Request' line context plus tracking), so PI-018 was next-available at close-out authoring time. TOCTOU pre-flight detected the collision; this payload rebased its planning item from PI-018 to PI-021 before applying. Sessions and decisions remained uncontested.

conversation_reference: Claude Code conversation on 05-20-26 working directly in the crmbuilder repo (no seed prompt). title: Fix engagement switching (in-process re-route) + connection/version introspection
topics_covered:
BUG. Switching engagements via the top-strip picker did not change displayed data. Root cause: the activation worker's switch relied on ServerLifecycle kill-and-relaunch of the API subprocess (per multi-engagement-architecture.md §4), but the API binds to one engagement's DB at spawn (env var CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH + lru-cached Settings). Against an externally-launched API ('crmbuilder-v2-api &', the documented dev workflow) terminate() is a no-op (the UI does not own that process) and start() short-circuits on a successful /health probe — so the switch silently re-attached to the old process on the old DB. A stale old-code API holding port 8765 during testing confirmed the failure mode live.

FIX (DEC-115). Switch now re-routes the live API in place via a new POST /admin/active-engagement, which calls the existing route_settings_to_engagement (DEC-110) to reset the settings + engine caches against the target DB — no subprocess restart, works whether the API is UI-owned or external. build_lifecycle_managers gained a client param; kill_api is a no-op when the client is wired; launch_api POSTs the re-route with a spawn fallback when the API is unreachable. On completion the main window refreshes the visible panel and marks the rest stale so they re-fetch. Verified end-to-end: CRMBUILDER→CBM flipped db_path (3.3 MB → 401 KB) and export_dir.

INTROSPECTION + VERSION SYSTEM (DEC-116). Added GET /admin/connection (bound DB path/exists/size, engagement code derived from the DB filename, export-dir state) and GET /admin/version (api_version from crmbuilder_v2.__version__; engagement + meta DB Alembic schema versions as current-revision vs chain-head + up_to_date flag). The FastAPI app version, previously a hardcoded '0.1.0' in two places, now reads __version__ (0.6.0). New migration/version_info.py reads the stamped revision from each DB's alembic_version table and the head from the chain. New Help → Connection Info… dialog surfaces all of it, querying the API so it reflects the server's live state rather than the UI process's startup get_settings() (which only routes at startup and would lag after a switch — the same reason the About dialog's db_path can be stale).

This discharges DEC-112's deferral of the diagnostic endpoint: the switching bug plus the operator's request to verify the running DB/version were the 'real need' DEC-112 said would justify building it. No schema migration (no new tables); the version surface reads existing Alembic state. ruff clean across all touched files.

Six decision records (DEC-117..122) and one planning item (PI-022) and seven references land via this payload's apply prompt. No source code modified; this is a pure planning conversation. No other PRDs touched.
conversation_reference: Claude.ai strategic scoping conversation across multiple working sessions in May 2026 (initial scoping early in May, verification and finalization on 05-20-26). The conversation had no formal kickoff prompt — opened ad-hoc as an exploration of how the planning-and-execution workflow functions in the V2 application's governance portion. The exploration surfaced a category-defining gap: the V2 governance database, while it tracks decisions, sessions, planning items, references, charter, status, risks, and topics as first-class records, does not track any of the operational artifacts the planning-and-execution workflow produces or consumes (kickoff prompts, workstream master plans, Product Requirements Documents, implementation plans, methodology guides, schema specifications, per-slice execution prompts, close-out payloads, deposit events, conversations, or workstreams). The gap directly contradicts the stated single-source-of-truth role of the database. The conversation worked through six principle-level questions to resolve how to close it. On 05-20-26, Doug returned to the conversation to verify the original draft kickoff prompt against the now-current state (after v0.5 and v0.6 had shipped and the methodology-schemas paper-test had completed). The verification revealed substantial drift: the draft's premise of pausing the methodology workstream was moot (it had completed); the version sequence assumption was stale; new architectural disciplines had emerged (strict reference vocabulary, envelope unwrap, per-engagement isolation); the draft was never committed. The updated kickoff prompt was authored against current state and committed at e1a0be4. No transcript preserved per DEC-025.
in_flight_at_end: The workstream-establishing planning conversation for the governance entity schema-design workstream opens against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-entity-schema-workstream-establishing-kickoff.md as the next step. That conversation produces the workstream master plan, the schema-spec methodology guide, six per-entity schema-design kickoff prompts, and its own close-out. After the workstream-establishing conversation closes, the six per-entity schema-design conversations run in order (workstream, conversation, reference book, work ticket, close-out payload, deposit event). After all six schema specifications are complete, a build-planning conversation consumes them and produces the integrating Product Requirements Document, implementation plan, and per-slice execution prompts. Target version for the resulting build is open — set by the build-planning conversation in coordination with the active version sequence at that time. The governance workstream operates in parallel to: (a) the multi-tenancy routing fix work in flight (slice B remaining); (b) the paper-test-flagged sub-domain hierarchy amendment (Planning Item 001 in the Cleveland Business Mentors engagement) which is a separate planning conversation; (c) the Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1 conversation which waits on (b). None of these block the governance workstream; the governance workstream does not block any of them. PI-022 (retroactive-migration question) opens for resolution at the workstream-establishing conversation or later — whether to backfill governance entity records for sessions, decisions, and prior workstreams already in the database, or only go forward from the moment the entities land.
title: Governance entity schema gap identified; six principle-level decisions reached; workstream-establishing kickoff prompt authored
topics_covered:
No seed prompt — this was an ad-hoc strategic scoping conversation opened without a kickoff. Topics worked through:

Gap identification. Doug opened with general questions about how the planning-and-execution workflow functions in V2. Walked through the workflow step by step: kickoff prompt creation, where prompts live, how they relate to sessions, how the workstream concept is captured. At each step, the question was asked whether the artifact in question was first-class in the governance database. The answer was uniformly no. Surfaced first for kickoff prompts (only referenced as free-text mentions in session records' artifacts_produced and topics_covered fields), then for workstreams (only an English word and a Markdown filename), then for the broader set of artifacts the workflow produces. Doug's principal observation: the single-source-of-truth principle is repeatedly violated wherever an operational artifact lives only on the filesystem with at-best a free-text mention in a session record.

Artifact inventory. Catalogued every artifact the planning-and-execution workflow produces or consumes, sorted by relationship to the governance database. Three categories fell out: (1) artifacts already first-class as typed records — the eight existing entity types (charter, status, decision, session, risk, planning item, topic, reference); (2) artifacts filesystem-only with free-text mentions in session records — initially seven types (workstream master plan, kickoff prompt, methodology guide, schema specification, Product Requirements Document, implementation plan, per-slice execution prompt), expanded to ten when the verification framing forced close-out payload, apply script, and the universal session-startup entry document into the same category; (3) artifacts filesystem-only with no database mention — empty after the corrections.

Lifecycle analysis. The ten category-two artifacts grouped into three lifecycle shapes: Shape A multi-consumer versioned reference document (workstream plan, methodology guide, schema specification, Product Requirements Document, implementation plan, session-startup entry document, apply script — seven of the ten), Shape B single-consumer seed document (kickoff prompt, per-slice execution prompt — two of the ten), Shape C single-use state-write package (close-out payload — one of the ten). The verification dimension forced a fourth shape into existence: Shape D apply event, a transactional record linking a payload to its application outcome and to the records it wrote.

Decision space. After lifecycle clarity, the decision space narrowed to two questions: how to handle the workflow artifacts (three purpose-built entity-type families per lifecycle shape, one generalized governed-artifact entity, or a hybrid), and whether to add a separate conversation entity for the unit of conversational work distinct from the after-the-fact session record.

Principle 1 — Three purpose-built entity-type families. Doug chose three purpose-built families over one generalized governed-artifact type. Reasoning: the families have meaningfully different lifecycles (long-lived versioned vs. single-use seed vs. single-use state-write); a single generalized entity would need an optional-field-heavy schema and a complex status enum accommodating all three shapes; per-family schemas are cleaner semantically.

Principle 2 — Two entities within the deposit bucket. The deposit bucket initially proposed as one entity holding both the close-out payload (the slip) and the deposit event (the apply record). Doug's framing about verification ("how do we know if the script/payload met the requirements if they are unavailable to a user using the DB?") forced separation: the payload is one record produced at conversation close; the deposit event is a separate record created at apply-time linking the payload to its outcome, the script version that ran, and the resulting records. Different lifecycles, different timing, different content. Two entities.

Principle 3 — Conversation as a first-class entity. The governance database knows about sessions, which are after-the-fact records. It has no concept of a planned-but-not-yet-happened conversation, a kickoff-drafted-but-not-yet-opened conversation, or an in-flight conversation. Doug chose to add a conversation entity representing the unit of conversational work through its full lifecycle (planned, kickoff-drafted, ready, in-flight, complete, cancelled, superseded). The session record continues to capture what happened at close; the conversation record carries the work unit through every state including the pre-session states.

Principle 4 — Workstream as a first-class entity. The workstream concept appears in filenames and free text but is not modeled. Doug chose to add a workstream entity representing a coherent line of related conversations. Workstreams have their own lifecycle (planned, in flight, complete) and let queries like 'show me every conversation in this workstream and the state of each' become answerable from the database. The nested-workstream question (whether a workstream can contain a sub-workstream) was deferred to the workstream entity's own schema-design conversation; it is a refinement, not a principle.

Principle 5 — Single source of truth coverage extension. Stated as the umbrella principle the four entity-introduction decisions serve: after the new entities land, queries like 'what is queued up next,' 'did this payload's apply succeed,' 'what version of the Product Requirements Document was in force at session NNN' become answerable from the database without filesystem scanning. Captures the design intent the gap violates today.

Principle 6 — Workstream order, governance workstream opens immediately. After the principles landed, the sequencing question: open a governance entity schema-design workstream now, or wait for other in-flight work. The methodology workstream had already completed; v0.5 engagement architecture had shipped; v0.6 styling had shipped; the paper test had completed with one BLOCKING finding (sub-domain hierarchy amendment to domain, recorded as Planning Item 001 in the Cleveland Business Mentors engagement); a multi-tenancy routing fix was in flight (Sessions 044 and 045 plus remaining slices). Doug chose to open the governance entity schema-design workstream immediately, in parallel to the in-flight work. Reasoning: the governance gap exists independently of any of the other workstreams; the Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1 waits on its own amendment regardless; the multi-tenancy fix is unrelated to governance entity scope; the governance workstream operates against the CRMBuilder dogfood engagement and does not touch other engagements' content.

Kickoff prompt drafting. After the six principles landed, Doug asked for the workstream-establishing kickoff prompt to be drafted in the same conversation. Three defaults baked in: workstream name 'governance entity schema-design workstream' (parallels 'methodology entity schema-design workstream'); the deposit bucket as two entities not one (per Principle 2); ordering of the six per-entity schema-design conversations by dependency — workstream first (most independent), then conversation (references workstream), then reference book (independent record but conversations link to it), then work ticket (references conversation), then close-out payload (produced by a conversation), then deposit event (references payload). Doug approved the draft.

Verification step on 05-20-26. After v0.5 and v0.6 completed and the paper test ran, Doug returned to verify the draft against current state. Key findings: the draft kickoff prompt was never committed (lived only in conversation history); the five-or-six original decisions were never recorded as formal database entries (the original conversation never closed out); the 'pause methodology workstream' framing was moot because the methodology workstream completed organically (SES-011 through SES-015 on 05-11/05-12 followed by v0.4 build); the version sequence assumption was stale (v0.5 and v0.6 had shipped); new architectural disciplines had emerged that did not exist or were not enforced at draft time (strict reference relationship vocabulary at crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/vocab.py with REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS plus _kinds_for_pair plus Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint; envelope unwrap pattern in apply scripts per the canonical SES-025 prompt; per-engagement isolation with engagements as a methodology entity introduced in v0.5). The kickoff prompt was rewritten against current state and committed at e1a0be4. The original six principles were preserved as 'six incoming principles' to be formalized as decisions at the close of the new workstream-establishing conversation — but this close-out (SES-046) formalizes them instead, since the new conversation will need them as predecessors.

Close-out artifact authoring. Following Doug's approval of the kickoff prompt and direction to handle the full close-out, this payload (ses_046.json) and the companion apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-046.md) were authored. Identifier check at close-out time: latest applied SES is SES-045 (multi-tenancy routing fix slice A build, 05-20-26) so SES-046 is next-available; latest DEC is DEC-116 (the diagnostic introspection decision in SES-045's payload) so DEC-117 through DEC-122 is next-available for six decisions; latest PI is PI-021 (the multi-tenancy routing PI surfaced in SES-044) so PI-022 is next-available for the retroactive-migration planning item. The apply prompt's pre-flight verifies these are still uncontested at run time per the TOCTOU mitigation pattern established by the SES-036 reconciliation precedent.

title: Governance entity schema-design workstream established; workstream master plan, methodology guide, and six per-entity schema-design kickoff prompts produced
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Establish the governance entity schema-design workstream and produce its scaffolding artifacts. Four categories of deliverable: (1) Six new decision records formalizing the six incoming principles below (numbers in the Decision 117 range; actual values resolved at conversation close); (2) PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-workstream-plan.md — workstream master plan; (3) PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md — methodology guide; (4) Six kickoff prompts under PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/, one per per-entity schema-design conversation."

Topics worked through:

1. Pre-flight resolution against the live database. The kickoff was authored under the assumption that the predecessor scoping conversation had not yet recorded the six principle-level decisions and the backfill planning item at this conversation's open. Pre-flight resolved that SES-046 had in fact closed out earlier the same day with DEC-117..122 and PI-022 already recorded. Treating those records as the workstream's foundation rather than re-recording reduced the deliverable count from four categories to three (master plan, methodology guide, six kickoff prompts) plus a session record.

2. Master plan drafting. Modeled on methodology-schema-workstream-plan.md (SES-011's analogue). Sections cover purpose, origin and foundation (referencing DEC-117..122 explicitly), scope (six entities under minimum-viable framing, with the six identifier-prefix working assumptions: WS, CONV, RB, WT, COP, DEP), methodology (eight-conversation workstream: this conversation + six schema-design + one build-planning), workstream structure with sequence diagram, independence (per DEC-122, does not gate on CBM PI-001 or multi-tenancy routing fix), bootstrap (the chicken-and-egg about designing workstream and conversation entities while operating inside a workstream and conversations that have no database records — resolved by deferring retroactive population to PI-022, executed after build ships), governance (no new decisions or planning items authored here; refers to DEC-117..122 and PI-022), architectural disciplines (vocabulary discipline per access/vocab.py, API envelope per the SES-025 fix, client-side identifier computation, per-engagement isolation, source-first relationship-kind naming per DEC-048, parent-prefix field naming per DEC-046), open questions, link-outs to companion documents, glossary.

3. Schema-spec methodology guide drafting. Modeled on methodology-entity-schema-spec-guide.md. Inherits parent-prefix field naming (DEC-046) and source-first relationship-kind naming (DEC-048) as policy for new entity types regardless of category — no methodology-only caveat, in contrast to the methodology guide's section 6 forward-only scope. PI-006 retrofit of existing governance entities remains tracked separately and untouched. Adds append-only as a first-class lifecycle option in section 3.4 (deposit event will likely need it; close-out payload may); methodology guide did not have this. Lifecycle posture inherited as 'prefer simple,' but conversation entity gets the full seven-state DEC-119 lifecycle explicitly as a tolerated exception. Section 6 cross-spec consistency table covers identifier prefix style, status field naming, status casing, relationship-kind naming, field naming, timestamp inheritance, soft-delete versus append-only, identifier auto-assignment, lifecycle preference, API envelope.

4. Six per-entity kickoff prompts drafted. Workstream order: workstream → conversation → reference book → work ticket → close-out payload → deposit event. Each follows the schema-design-kickoff-domain.md model from the methodology workstream: task, context (why this entity at this position), what the entity needs to host with real grounding examples from the current project state, read-first list, architectural questions likely to arise, working style, pre-flight, governance at close, what the conversation does NOT do. Grounding examples drawn from real project artifacts: actual workstreams (methodology, v0.5 engagement-management, v0.6 styling, multi-tenancy routing fix, CBM paper test, this one), actual reference books (PRDs v0.1..v0.6, implementation plans, master plans, methodology guides, the conduct framework, the multi-engagement architecture document), actual work tickets (the methodology workstream's per-entity kickoffs and this workstream's seven kickoffs, the Claude Code apply prompts), actual close-out payload files at close-out-payloads/. The deposit-event kickoff (sixth) has one extra deliverable at close — the build-planning conversation's kickoff prompt — called out explicitly so it is not lost in closeout.

5. Identifier prefix working assumptions named but not locked. WS, CONV, RB, WT, COP, DEP carried through the kickoff prompts as working assumptions. Each per-entity conversation has first refusal at lock-in; the spec guide's section 6 collision list will be amended as each conversation closes.

6. Mid-conversation correction (committed separately). Initial drafts of the workstream plan and deposit-event kickoff stated that this conversation authors the retroactive-backfill planning item. Pre-flight had already established PI-022 exists; the claim slipped through into drafting. Five passages in the workstream plan and one passage in the deposit-event kickoff updated to cite PI-022 by identifier and attribute its authorship to SES-046. Two follow-up file changes, one follow-up commit.

7. Bootstrap framing explicit in master plan section 7. This workstream designs the workstream and conversation entities, but the conversations doing the designing are themselves conversations inside a workstream that has no records. Resolution: during the workstream, the session record on the append-only sessions table remains the only governance record per conversation per DEC-013; the workstream itself has no database record during its lifetime; retroactive population is PI-022's responsibility, executed after the build ships. Each schema-design conversation designs without depending on backfill ordering, but the schemas must support retroactive population (records insertable with historical timestamps and complete status).

8. Eight artifacts produced over four local commits, pushed to origin/main after sign-off: governance-schema-workstream-plan.md, governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md, six schema-design-kickoff-{entity}.md files. Plus one corrective commit for the PI-022 framing.

artifacts_produced:
Two files committed to PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ in the crmbuilder repo during the conversation, plus three more produced at close:

During the conversation:

(1) governance-schema-specs/workstream.md (new, v1.0, 393 lines, committed at b981c1f). First per-entity schema specification in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Conforms to governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md. Establishes the workstream entity type for V2 storage with nine fields plus seven timestamps (three inherited base + four per-status lifecycle), five-status lifecycle with truly terminal terminals and supersession-requires-edge rule, no foreign-key columns (parent-child relationships go through refs per Decision 1), references-edge master plan linkage to reference_book with new kind workstream_planned_in_reference_book, references-edge supersession to another workstream reusing the existing generic supersedes kind, standard 8-endpoint API surface, default UI layout with conditional lifecycle-timestamp rendering. Sixteen testable acceptance criteria captured. Vocabulary additions named for build-planning aggregation: REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS += {conversation_belongs_to_workstream, workstream_planned_in_reference_book}; ENTITY_TYPES += {workstream, conversation, reference_book}; matching _kinds_for_pair clauses; matching Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint.

(2) governance-schema-specs/ directory created in the same commit (b981c1f) as workstream.md. The directory is the home for the remaining five governance entity schema specifications.

At conversation close:

(3) close-out-payloads/ses_048.json (new — this payload). One session (SES-048), six decisions (DEC-123..128), zero planning items, seven references (six decided_in plus one is_about SES-048 → PI-022).

(4) prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-048.md (new — this payload's apply prompt). Idempotent apply targeting the CRMBUILDER engagement.

Via this payload's apply prompt, the following records land in the CRMBUILDER engagement database:

(5) DEC-123 — workstream identifier prefix and format (WS, WS-NNN, two-letter form acceptable without locking the cross-spec norm).

(6) DEC-124 — workstream-to-conversation via references-table edge per DEC-120's decision text; new relationship kind conversation_belongs_to_workstream; cross-spec precedent of references-edge over foreign-key for parent-child governance relationships.

(7) DEC-125 — workstream lifecycle: five statuses including superseded with truly terminal terminals and supersession-requires-edge rule reusing the existing generic supersedes kind.

(8) DEC-126 — workstream field inventory with per-status lifecycle timestamps; cross-spec precedent of per-status lifecycle timestamps for workflow-shaped lifecycles.

(9) DEC-127 — workstream flat, no nesting in this release; retrofit path documented as a references-edge addition rather than self-FK column.

(10) DEC-128 — workstream master plan linkage via references-edge with new kind workstream_planned_in_reference_book; standard 8-endpoint API surface with no deviations; default UI layout with conditional lifecycle-timestamp rendering; default soft-delete posture; sixteen acceptance criteria captured.

(11) Six decided_in references (DEC-123..128 → SES-048) plus one is_about reference (SES-048 → PI-022, mirroring SES-047's pattern of linking the schema-design conversation to the retroactive-backfill planning item).

No source code modified — this is a planning conversation producing schema specifications only. Vocabulary changes, schema migrations, and access-layer methods are aggregated by the build-planning conversation after all six schemas exist.
conversation_reference: Claude.ai schema-design conversation for the workstream entity — first per-entity schema-design conversation in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Opened against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-workstream.md (the kickoff committed as part of SES-047's artifacts at 369f32e on 05-20-26). The conversation produced one deliverable per the kickoff and the schema-spec methodology guide's template: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/workstream.md (393 lines, committed at b981c1f), creating the governance-schema-specs/ directory in the same commit. Architectural discussion was driven by the operating preferences for ARCHITECTURE mode (surface only decisions that pass the two-part test of real downstream impact and meaningfully different viable options; decide routine items as drafts; consequential decisions use the eight-element template). Four consequential decisions were surfaced and resolved via the template; five routine items were drafted and confirmed in two batches. The conversation also surfaced two pre-existing drifts between the kickoff prompt's working assumptions and DEC-120's explicit decision text — both resolved in favour of DEC-120 (foundation decisions are binding unless cause to override). No transcript preserved per DEC-025.
in_flight_at_end: Next workstream conversation: conversation entity schema design (second per-entity schema-design conversation, of six). Opens against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-conversation.md. That conversation produces governance-schema-specs/conversation.md and inherits the three cross-spec precedents locked by SES-048: references-edge over foreign-key for parent-child governance relationships, per-status lifecycle timestamps for workflow-shaped lifecycles, and terminal-states-are-terminal discipline. The conversation entity is structurally the workstream entity's most direct downstream consumer — every conversation record will carry an outbound conversation_belongs_to_workstream edge to its workstream per the relationship kind named in this conversation's decisions. After conversation.md lands, the remaining four schema-design conversations follow in workstream order (reference book, work ticket, close-out payload, deposit event). After all six schema specifications exist, the build-planning conversation opens against governance-schema-build-planning-kickoff.md (to be drafted at the close of the deposit-event conversation per SES-047's in-flight notes) and produces the integrating Product Requirements Document, implementation plan, per-slice build prompts, and the consolidated vocab.py update aggregating relationship-kind additions from all six specs. Multi-tenancy routing fix slice B and the Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1 conversation continue in parallel as independent workstreams per DEC-122.
title: Workstream entity schema designed; first per-entity governance schema specification produced
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Design the `workstream` entity type schema for V2's storage layer. Drive a structured architectural discussion that produces one deliverable: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/workstream.md — the complete schema specification per the template in governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md. The directory does not yet exist; this conversation creates it. Cadence matches the methodology workstream's schema-design conversations and the user-interface planning conversations: structured architectural discussion driven one decision at a time, building toward the specification section by section. At conversation close: decisions written via direct API as decision records; any deferred items written as planning items; one session record written by Doug through the V2 desktop New Session dialog per the session-record-at-close pattern."

Topics worked through:

1. Pre-flight orientation. Sparse-cloned the crmbuilder repo and read the eight-item reading list named in the kickoff: crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md, governance-schema-workstream-plan.md, governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md, the six foundation decisions DEC-117..122 from db-export/decisions.json, SES-046 and SES-047 from db-export/sessions.json, multi-engagement-architecture.md, crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/vocab.py, and methodology-schema-specs/domain.md as the structural exemplar. API health check (against the conversation's sandbox localhost, not Doug's machine) and pytest run (sparse checkout doesn't include tests/) were flagged as not meaningfully runnable in the conversation environment; design-only conversation, so neither gates.

2. Two concerns surfaced before opening Decision 1. (a) The kickoff's working assumption for workstream-to-conversation linkage was 'direct foreign key on conversation table,' but DEC-120's decision text explicitly says 'Conversations belong to a workstream via a relationship in the references table.' Drift; DEC-120 binds. (b) The kickoff said three statuses (planned, in_flight, complete) with cancelled and superseded flagged as open, while DEC-120 itself names four statuses including cancelled, and DEC-117 references 'three-status lifecycle posture' as the inherited methodology pattern. Drift; DEC-120 binds for cancelled (in by default), superseded genuinely open.

3. Decision 1 — workstream-to-conversation relationship via references-table edge. Eight-element template. Confirmed DEC-120's references-edge mechanism over the kickoff's working-assumption foreign-key. Cross-spec precedent locked: references-edge over foreign-key for parent-child governance relationships. New relationship kind named: `conversation_belongs_to_workstream` (source-first per DEC-048). Approved Option B.

4. Decision 2 — status lifecycle, five values including superseded. Eight-element template. Confirmed cancelled per DEC-120 with no debate. Resolved superseded as in (five statuses total) for parity with DEC-119's conversation entity (which has superseded as a status), with truly-terminal terminal states (no transitions out of any terminal; reactivation is conceptually a new workstream). Supersession reuses the existing generic `supersedes` reference kind for the (workstream, workstream) pair; access-layer rule requires an outgoing supersedes edge when status is superseded. Approved Option A.

5. Identifier prefix — drafted as WS, not surfaced as a separate decision. Two-letter form acceptable per the spec guide's 2-5 letter range; matches PI's short-prefix posture; doesn't conflict with the existing collision list. Downstream conversations have their own first refusal on their working prefixes.

6. Decision 3 — nested workstreams. Eight-element template. Resolved the deferred-from-DEC-120 question: flat catalog, no nesting in this release. Zero observed need in the project's operating history; DEC-120's 'if nested workstreams are admitted' phrasing leans this way; user-interface cost of hierarchical rendering is real; the retrofit path is documented as a references-edge addition (not self-FK column) so the workstream table is not touched if nesting becomes needed later. Cleveland Business Mentors Planning Item 001 (sub-domain hierarchy on the methodology `domain` entity) is NOT pre-decided by this — the methodology workstream's PI-001 conversation makes its own call with this conversation's reasoning available but not binding. Approved Option A.

7. Decision 4 — lifecycle timestamps. Eight-element template. Per-status lifecycle timestamps adopted: workstream_started_at, _completed_at, _cancelled_at, _superseded_at (four columns, all nullable, server-set on the matching transition). No planned_at column (redundant with created_at). Diverges from the methodology entities' base-timestamps-only pattern; the divergence is appropriate because methodology entities have propose-verify lifecycles while workstream has a workflow-shaped lifecycle that benefits from queryable per-status timestamps. Cross-spec precedent locked: per-status lifecycle timestamps for workflow-shaped governance lifecycles (conversation entity likely inherits this). Approved Option B.

8. Routine items drafted and approved as a single batch — master plan linkage (references-edge to a reference_book record with new kind `workstream_planned_in_reference_book`, provisional naming, reference_book conversation may refine, build-planning reconciles); soft-delete posture (default with restore — workstream is organizing metadata not transactional fact, so append-only is inappropriate; soft-delete is distinct from cancelled status, which is lifecycle outcome rather than administrative removal); API surface (standard 8-endpoint set with no deviations); user-interface considerations (default layout with two natural additions documented in the spec — conditional lifecycle-timestamp rendering on the detail pane showing only non-null timestamps, and supersession-requires-edge UX where the user must add the outgoing supersedes edge before committing status to superseded).

9. Spec drafting in one pass per spec guide section 4.3. governance-schema-specs/workstream.md drafted following the structural shape of methodology-schema-specs/domain.md, with all nine required subsections of section 3 substantively filled. 16 testable acceptance criteria captured (spec guide gate requires 10). Section 3.8 categorized by build / backfill / future tags per the spec guide convention. Section 3.9 cross-references DEC-117..122 as foundation decisions and DEC-046, DEC-048, DEC-013, DEC-115/116 as inherited conventions. Committed at b981c1f after a small fix to the acceptance-criterion-1 column count (10 → 13, correcting the field-section sum).

10. Close-out artifacts authored. After Doug's confirmation, this payload (ses_048.json) and the companion apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-048.md) were drafted in one pass. Identifier check at close-out: latest applied SES is SES-047 so SES-048 is next-available; latest DEC is DEC-122 so DEC-123..128 is next-available for the six decisions; no new planning items (PI-022 already covers retroactive backfill including this workstream's records).

artifacts_produced:
One file committed to PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ in the crmbuilder repo during the conversation, plus two more produced at close:

During the conversation:

(1) governance-schema-specs/conversation.md (new, v1.0, 459 lines, committed at 6580a32). Second per-entity schema specification in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Conforms to governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md. Establishes the conversation entity type for V2 storage with six content/classification fields plus nine timestamps (three inherited base + six per-status lifecycle), seven-status lifecycle (planned → kickoff_drafted → ready → in_flight → one of complete / cancelled / superseded) with truly terminal terminals inherited from workstream's precedent plus a forward-only planning lifecycle and two access-layer rules (complete-requires-session-edge, supersession-requires-edge). No foreign-key columns (every relationship in refs per Decision 1 and the inherited precedent). Four references-edge kinds registered: conversation_belongs_to_workstream (declared inbound in workstream.md, registered here per the deferral note), conversation_records_session (new), conversation_opens_against_work_ticket (new, tentative), conversation_succeeds_conversation (new, establishing the typed-sequencing precedent). Standard 8-endpoint API surface with two new list-endpoint filters (workstream_identifier, status). Default UI layout with a fifth master-pane column (Workstream) and matching toolbar filter combos. Eighteen testable acceptance criteria captured. Vocabulary additions named for build-planning aggregation: REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS += {conversation_belongs_to_workstream, conversation_records_session, conversation_opens_against_work_ticket, conversation_succeeds_conversation}; ENTITY_TYPES += {conversation, work_ticket}; four _kinds_for_pair clauses; matching Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint.

At conversation close:

(2) close-out-payloads/ses_049.json (new — this payload). One session (SES-049), six decisions (DEC-129..134), zero planning items, seven references (six decided_in plus one is_about SES-049 → PI-022).

(3) prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-049.md (new — this payload's apply prompt). Idempotent apply targeting the CRMBUILDER engagement.

Via this payload's apply prompt, the following records land in the CRMBUILDER engagement database:

(4) DEC-129 — conversation identifier prefix CONV, four-letter form matching PROC/CRMC precedent.

(5) DEC-130 — conversation-to-session via references-table edge with new kind conversation_records_session per the inherited cross-spec precedent from DEC-124; resolves the kickoff prompt's drift toward an FK column on the sessions table in favour of the precedent; preserves the append-only sessions table shape per DEC-013.

(6) DEC-131 — conversation lifecycle: seven statuses with truly terminal terminals, forward-only planning lifecycle, complete-requires-session-edge rule, supersession-requires-edge rule inherited from workstream verbatim.

(7) DEC-132 — conversation-to-kickoff (work_ticket) via references-table edge with tentative new kind conversation_opens_against_work_ticket; required-when rule binds the edge to statuses past kickoff_drafted; work_ticket conversation may refine; build-planning reconciles.

(8) DEC-133 — conversation_succeeds_conversation introduced for predecessor-successor chaining within a workstream; diverges from workstream's defer-sequencing posture on frequency grounds; establishes new cross-spec precedent that typed sequencing edges are introduced when entity-family frequency justifies.

(9) DEC-134 — conversation field inventory (six content/classification fields plus nine timestamps); list-endpoint filters for workstream_identifier and status; master-pane Workstream column as fifth column; default soft-delete with restore distinct from cancelled status; eighteen acceptance criteria captured.

(10) Six decided_in references (DEC-129..134 → SES-049) plus one is_about reference (SES-049 → PI-022, mirroring SES-047 and SES-048's pattern of linking the schema-design conversation to the retroactive-backfill planning item).

No source code modified — this is a planning conversation producing schema specifications only. Vocabulary changes, schema migrations, and access-layer methods are aggregated by the build-planning conversation after all six schemas exist.
